pages.title: Overview
pages.body:
A single rotating cube demonstrates the essential building blocks of an Artlab scene: geometry, a PBR material, a three-light rig, shadow casting, edge wireframe overlay, a ground plane, and frame-rate-independent animation.

Navigate through the pages below to walk through each section of this file.

pages.title: Cube Mesh & Edge Overlay
pages.body:
A BoxGeometry is paired with a MeshStandardMaterial that uses a dim emissive color to prevent the unlit face from going fully black. An EdgesGeometry derived from the same box is rendered as LineSegments and attached as a child of the cube mesh, so it rotates with it automatically.

· emissive keeps dark faces readable under strong directional light
· EdgesGeometry reuses the existing geometry — no extra vertex data needed
· mesh.add(edges) parents the wireframe so transforms stay in sync

pages.title: Camera, Shadows & Lights
pages.body:
The camera is placed at a three-quarter view angle. Shadow mapping is enabled on the renderer using PCFSoft for smoother penumbras. Three lights approximate a studio rig:

· AmbientLight — raises the shadow floor so nothing goes pure black
· DirectionalLight — the primary sun, positioned upper-right with shadow casting on
· PointLight — a blue-tinted rim from behind-left that adds depth and color contrast

pages.title: Ground Plane
pages.body: A large PlaneGeometry rotated flat receives shadows from the cube. Its near-black color and roughness:1 material keep it visually neutral so the cube and its shadow are the focus. The plane is not stored in a variable because it never needs to be referenced after setup.

pages.title: Animation in update()
pages.body: Each frame, the cube spins on both X and Y axes using dt (delta-time in seconds) so motion is frame-rate-independent. A sine wave on ctx.elapsed drives a gentle vertical bob, giving the cube a floating, breathing quality.

pages.title: Teardown
pages.body: teardown() explicitly removes the cube mesh from the scene. Because the edge overlay and all lights were added via ctx.add(), the Artlab runtime handles their disposal automatically when the example unloads.
